key_insight: Defects and canted multi-domain structure suppress the super-diffusive transport of an active Brownian particle, turning the expected nematic Goldstone-mode-driven \(t\ln t\) enhancement into ordinary constrained motion.
engineering_relevance: This shows that microstructural defect design in liquid-crystal-like media can be used to tune active-matter mobility and transport regimes.
method: Generalized Lebwohl-Lasher (GLL) model to generate isotropic, nematic, and canted host phases.
Off-lattice overdamped Langevin dynamics for coupled active-particle transport.
Quantified motion using step-size distributions (SSDs), mean-square displacement (MSD), and Hurst exponents.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports left-skewed exponential and Rayleigh-like SSDs in the uniform nematic phase, with transverse \(t\ln t\) super-diffusion disappearing when defects or domain frustration are present.
watch_item: The suppression claim depends on idealized model assumptions and may change with finite-size effects, defect density, or stronger hydrodynamic coupling.
paper_title: Suppression of Active Super-Diffusion: Impact of String Defects and Canted Multi-Domains
